cards.back:
Facial angiofibroma 
Sign of tuberous sclerosis

cards.front: What is this? What is it a sign of?

Ash leaf patch
Tuberous sclerosis

cards.front: What is this? What is it a sign of?
cards.back:
Shagreen patch (differs from ash leaf patch in that it's on connective tissue) 
Sign of tuberous sclerosis

cards.front: What is this finding and what is it associated with?
cards.back:
Cafe au lait macules
NF1

cards.front: What does this finding signify?
cards.back:
Cutaenous/mucosal telangectasias =
Hereditary hemorrhagic telangectasia

cards.front: What is this?
cards.back: Dermal sinus tract

cards.front: What is this and what is it associated with?
cards.back:
Hairy patch (hypertrichosis) = more
likely diastematomyelia/split cord

cards.front: What is this?
cards.back: Lumbar lipoma = lipomyelomeningocele

cards.front: What is this and what is it associated with in peds/adults?
cards.back:
Cherry red spot:

Peds = Sphingolipidoses
(Niemann-Pick/ Gaucher/ Tay-Sachs/ GM1 gangliosidosis)

Adult = central retinal artery occlusion

cards.front: What is this? And what disease process is associated?
cards.back:
Gilliat-Sumner hand:
Thenar+hypothenar
wasting, thoracic outlet syndrome

cards.front: What is this sign and what is it associated with?
cards.back:
Warternberg sign = ulnar compression @ cubital tunnel (2/2 weakness of adduction of
pinky)

cards.front: What is this sign and what is it associated with?
cards.back:
Froment sign = ulnar compression @ cubital tunnel (2/2 weakness of adductor
pollicis)

cards.front:
What is this sign and what is it associated with? 
(This occurs whne the patient tries to make a fist)
cards.back:
Hand of benediction = proximal median
nerve 2/2 weakness of d2-3 flexors

cards.front:
What is this and what is it associated with? 
occurs when trying to open the hand
cards.back:
Ulnar claw = ulnar compression @ cubital tunnel (2/2 weakness of d4/5
extensors)

Similar to Klumpke’s palsy 8/1

cards.front: What is this sign and what weakness is it associated with?
cards.back:
OK sign = AIN palsy 2/2 weakness of
FDP+FPL

cards.front: What is this sign and what is it associated with?
cards.back:
Partial wrist drop w/ absent finger
extension = PIN palsy

cards.front: What is this sign and what is it associated with?
cards.back: Erb’s palsy/waiter’s tip – C5/6

cards.front: Review APD. When this this occur?
cards.back:
Marcus Gun pupil / RAPD = CN2 lesion / MS
/ NMO

cards.front: What is this called and what is it a sign of?
cards.back:
Adie’s pupil = sluggish/absent response
(constriction) to light, but better response to accommodation. 2/2 ciliary
ganglion degen

Window frame spine / sandwich vertebrae=
osteopetrosis

Myxopapillary ependymoma (sausage shape @
conus)
Giant cell tumor
Sacral chordoma

cards.front: What is this called in general? What does it signify in adults vs peds?
cards.back:
Vertebra plana:

Adult: plasmacytoma/myeloma/osteoporotic fx

Peds: eosinophilic granuloma

Polka dot sign
Benign hemangioma
Osteoid osteoma (because less than 2cm)
Aneurysmal bone cyst

Bamboo spine + SI joint inflammation =
Ankylosing spondilitis

DISH (diffuse idiopathic skeletal
hyperostosis)
OPLL

Charcot joint = neuropathic
osteoarthropathy of shoulder joint w humeral head erosion; suspect presence of
syrinx/Chiari 1. Also
a/w
DM/syphilis/MS
Benign venous angioma = can be associated with cav mal
Benign capillary telangectasia

Carotid-cavernous fistula (CCF), see
dilated sup ophthalmic vein

Tuberous sclerosis, see hyperdense
periventricular nodules
Tram track calcification = sturge weber
Paget’s disease aka Osteitis deformans
eosinophilic granuloma
Dermoid, purely lytic. Prefer the ant fontanelle/suture line

Epidermoid, smooth sclerotic borders.
Less preference for suture lines, parietal bone most common. Restrict on DWI

Pott’s puffy tumor – frontal sinusitis +
osteomyelitis
CPA = epidermoid cyst
hemangiopericytoma

Clival chordoma: midline, purely lytic on
CT, strong/heterogenous C+

DDx: chondrosarcoma: eccentric/petroclival,
lytic but w internal chondroid matrix on CT, very similar MR characteristics
only *bright on ADC*
Glomus jugulare = paraganglioma

Lhermitte Duclos = Dysplastic cerebellar
gangliocytoma
Central neurocytoma
SEGA in a TS patient
Desmoplastic infantile glioma (DIG)

Dysembryoplastic
neuroepithelial tumor (DNET), bubble appearance
Ganglioglioma, Ca+ cyst w C+ nodule
CO poisoning

Persistent trigeminal artery (from
cavernous ICA, most common)

Otic = from petrous ICA (very rare, first
anastomosis to regress)

Hyoglossal = from cervical ICA (C1-3), comes
through hypoglossal canal

Proatlantal = from cervical ICA/ECA
Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma

subependymoma 
non enhancing 4th ventricle

Choroid plexus papilloma – cauliflower,
avid C+, prefers lateral vent, but can occur in 4th also

Tentorial meningioma fed by artery of bernasconi
& cassinari
(MHT)
Lipoma
Colloid cyst

Fibro muscular dysplasia = beads on
string

Lyme disease, Bell’s palsy, CN7
enhancement
HSV1 encephalitis

Septooptic dysplasia = no septum pellucidum, thin
optic nerves

Grey matter heterotopia, migration
disorder
Schizencephaly
Lissencephaly
Polymicrogyria
Dandy Walker
neuromyelitis optica
Tigroid MRI – metachromic leukodystrophy, Pelizaeus-Merzbacher
adrenoleukodystrophy
Alexander disease (leukodystrophy)

panda face
wilson disease

‘Eye of the tiger’ – iron deposits in Hallervorden-Spatz
disease

Hot crossed bun sign = multisystem
atrophy
Hummingbird sign – midbrain atrophy à progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP
Hockeystick sign = Creutzfeld Jacobs (CJD)

Cognard type1 
dAVF
Cognard type 2 glomus AVM

Wernicke’s encephalopathy – signal
abnormalities in b/l
thalami + mammillary bodies
Hypothalamic hamartoma

Adamantinomatous
craniopharyngioma-wet keratin, palisading epithelium,
stellate reticulum

Pituitary adenoma: Acidophiles
(40%: PRL, GH, FSH/LH), basophils (10%: ACTH, TSH), null cells (50%)

Dermoid:oily/chol
fluid, hair shafts, glands

Epidermoid -dry
keratin

Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma (PXA) - Pleomorphic
astrocytes and xanthomatous
fat cells, S100+
SEGA: a/w tuberous sclerosis

Ganglioglioma: GFAP,
synaptophysin,
abnormal neurons, some binucleate

DNET (dysembryoplastic neuro-epithelial tumor): Soap
bubble tumor, Floating neurons . columns
of axons lined by monotonous oligodendroglioma-like cells with intervening
“floating” neurons (cortical neurons in mucin pools)

Central neurocytoma -Monotonous
uniform cells w perinuclear halo ,
Synaptophysin
Hypothalamic hamartoma

GBM: pseudopalisading
necrosis, microvascular proliferation, high
mitotic count, nuclear polymorph
Astrocytoma

Oligodendroglioma - egg
yolk cytoplasm, chicken wire vessels

Pseudolaminar
necrosis – s/p ischemia

Juvenile pilocytic astrocytoma (JPA) - Biphasic pattern (loose/dense
microcytic)
Rosenthal fibers – JPA, Alexander disease

Homer Wright rosettes – medulloblastoma,
neuroblastoma

Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes –
medulloblastoma/ retinoblastoma/pinealblastoma,
centrally projecting cytoplasmic extensions from tumor cells

Medulloblastoma = small,
round undifferentiated cells with mild to moderate nuclear pleomorphism

True rosettes (around central canal,
left) and perivascular pseudo-rosettes (right) - ependymoma

Hemangioblastoma - +Vimentin,
-EMA

Subependymoma - Islands
of blue in sea of pink
Choroid plexus papilloma - +S100/Vimentin/cytokeratin

Verocay bodies - palisading
nuclei around eosinophillic
core - Schwannoma
Meningioma – whorls and psammoma bodies
Schwannoma – Antoni A & B, school of fish, S100+

Myoxopapillary
ependymoma = pseudo-papillary
structures with cuboidal ependymal cells centered around mucin-rich
perivascular spaces and central vessels forming perivascular pseudo-rosettes.
Myxoid material is commonly found adjacent to tumor cells.

Hemangiopericytoma – staghorn vessels, +Vimentin + CD34, -EMA
(unlike meningioma),

Chordoma - Sheets/cords
of large vacuolated cells (physaliphorous
or bubbly cells) surrounded by mucin; +EMA +cytokeratin
(ectoderm) +S100
brachyury (mesenchymal/neural
crest)
Paraganglioma - zellballen

Langerhans cell histiocytosis - Multinucleated
giant cells /
granulomas

Fibrous dysplasia -Woven
bone, thickened/sclerotic bone with ground grass diploe

Pineocytoma - Rosette-like
clusters (almost Homer Wright)

Germinoma - large
polygonal cells, prominent nucleoli
Schuller duval body = Yolk sac tumor (endodermal sinus)
ATRT

CNS lymphoma – perivascular lymphocytic
infiltrate
Pilocytic astrocytoma smear

Colloid cyst - mucin
filled, +/-ciliated columnal cells, PAS+
goblet cells

Rathke’s cleft cyst - watery
mucous fluid w columnar/cuboid epithelium
Red neurons = ischemia

Cavernous malformation - Endothelium
lined caverns w/o mature vessel wall
AVM
Capillary telangectasia

Chondrosarcoma - S100+,
EMA-,
biphasic pattern: spindle shaped cells + well-diff cartilage

Osteochondroma = benign
exophytic lesions that may be sessile or peduncular

Giant cell tumor of bone – lytic sacral
tumor

Neurogenic muscle atrophy:multiple
nuclear clumps or nuclear bags.

Possible causes: peripheraly
neuropath, ALS, SMA, Hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy (Charcot Marie
Tooth disease)

Lafora bodies – AR glycogen storage
myopathy
Bunina bodies - ALS

Cowdry bodies = intranuclear
inclusions w surrounding halo (nonspecific, also seen in CMV/HSV/VZV, rubella,
HIV

SSPE Subacute
sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE)

Alzheimer's disease

o Intracytoplasmic
tau protein neurofibrillary tangles (Bielschowsky
silver stain)

o Extracellular
amyloid-B neurotic plaques

o Hirano
bodies

Pick bodies = Fronto-temporal
dementia (FTD, Pick)

Spongiform
encephalitis
- Creutzfeld-Jakob

Lewy body (a-synuclein intracytoplasmic
inclusions) = PD, LBD

Perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate –
viral encephalitis

Negri body (cytoplasmic inclusion) + Babes
nodule (cellular grouping)

Aspergillus - Acute-angle
branching segmented hyphae & conidiophores

Candida - pseudohyphae
(meth silver stain)

Mucor -Broad
branching hyphae, non-seg
Cryptococcus
Coccidiomycosis spherule w endospore
Cystercercosis
Toxoplasmosis – spherule w tachyzoite
Charcot marie tooth onion bulb neuropathy
neurofibroma
PMNST
Gaucher cell
Niemann pick foam cell

Globoid cell PAS+ multinucleated – Krabbe
disease (leukodystrophy)

Alzheimer type 2 astrocyte = Canavan
disease, hepatic encephalopathy

Zebra bodies = large
lamellar inclusions of dermatan and heparan sulfate; a/w

cards.front:
What structures pass through the superior orbital fissure? 
(There are 6 groups of things to name)
cards.back:
CN III, IV, VI

CN V1: all three branches (nasociliary, frontal, lacrimal)

Sympathetic fibers from ICA plexus

Middle meningeal artery (orbital branch)

Lacrimal artery (recurrent meningeal branch)

Superior ophthalmic vein

cards.front: What structures pass through the jugular foramen?
cards.back:
Internal jugular vein 
Glossopharyngeal nerve
Vagus nerve
Accessory nerve
Inferior petrosal sinus 
Posterior meningeal artery

cards.front: What structures pass through the inferior orbital fissure?
cards.back:
CN V2

Zygomatic nerve

Maxillary nerve (pterygopalatine branch)

Infraorbital artery and vein

Inferior ophthalmic vein

cards.front: What structures pass through the foramen rotundum?
cards.back: CN V2 (mnemonic: CN rotwondum)

cards.front: What structures pass through the foramen ovale?
cards.back:
CN V3 (mnemonic: ovalee = CN three)

Lesser superficial petrosal nerve

cards.front: What structures pass through the carotid canal?
cards.back:
Sympathetic nerves

ICA

cards.front: What structures pass through the supraorbital foramen?
cards.back: CN V1: supraorbital vessels and nerve

cards.front: What structures pass through the infraorbital foramen?
cards.back: CN V2: infraorbital vessels and nerve

cards.front: What structures pass through Dorello's canal?
cards.back: CN VI: not a true foramen

cards.front: What is the optic strut?
cards.back: Bone between the optic foramen and superior orbital fissure

cards.front: What is the falciform ligament?
cards.back: Dura extending between anterior clinoid and planum sphenoidale, covering the optic nerve

cards.front: What are the borders and contents of the clinoidal triangle?
cards.back:
Medial: CN II
Lateral: CNIII
Base: Tentorium 
Conents: Clinoidal segment of the ICA

cards.front: What are the borders and contents of the oculomotor (hakuba) triangle?
cards.back:
Medial: interclinoid fold
Lateral: anterior petroclinoid fold
Base: posterior petroclinoid fold 
Contents: CN III

cards.front: What are the borders of the supratrochlear triangle?
cards.back:
Medial: CN III
Lateral: CN IV
Base: Tentorium 
Contents: MHT

cards.front: What are the contents and borders of the anteromedial triangle?
cards.back:
Medial: CN V1
Lateral: CN V2 
Base: Line btwn the SOF and foramen rotundum 
Contents: sphenoid sinus, CN VI

cards.front: What are the contents and borders of the anterolateral triangle?
cards.back:
Medial: CN V2 
Lateral: CN V3 
Base: Line between foramen rotundum and ovale 
Contents: none

cards.front: What are the structures that pass above and through the annulus of Zinn?
Do the optic nerve and ophthlamic artery pass through the annulus of Zinn?
cards.back: Yes, they enter orbit through optic canal and pass through annulus of Zinn

cards.front: Do the superior and inferior ophthalmic veins pass through the annulus of Zinn?
cards.back:
Superior and inferior ophthalmic veins: pass through the superior and inferior orbital fissures, respectively. 
Do not pass through the anulus of Zinn.

cards.front: What does CN III superior division innervate?
cards.back: CN III superior division: levator palpebrae superioris and superior rectus

cards.front: What does CN III inferior division innervate?
cards.back: CN III inferior division: inferior rectus, medial rectus, and inferior oblique

cards.front: What does CN IV innervate?
cards.back: CN IV: superior oblique

cards.front: What does CN VI innervate?
cards.back: Lateral rectus

cards.front: What does Muller's muscle innervate?
cards.back: Eyelid retractor, sympathetic

cards.front:
Parasympathetic innervation of the eye 
1. begins at the Edinger–Westphal nucleus 
2. transmitting via the third nerve → ciliary ganglion behind the eye → short ciliary nerves → ciliary muscle (for accommodation) and iris sphincter

What does the parasympathetic innervation cause - miosis or mydriasis?
cards.back: Miosis

cards.front:
Sympathetic innervation of the eye: 

from the T1 level → sympathetic chain → superior cervical ganglion, up

along the carotid artery to the small vessels, and then as the long and short ciliary nerves to the eye’s radial iris fibers to Müller’s muscle of the eyelid and weakly to the ciliary muscle.

Does this cause miosis or mydriasis?
cards.back: Mydriasis

cards.front: What is the argyll robertson pupil?
cards.back:
No response to light
Accomodation is intact 

Seen in syphilis "Doesn't react but accomodates" from sketchy micro

cards.front: Describe horner's syndrome? What causes it and what are the symptoms (x5)?
cards.back: caused by impaired sympathetic input resulting in miosis, ptosis (i.e., sympathetic nerves innervate the smooth muscle of the eyelid), anhidrosis, enophthalmos, and dilated facial vessels.

cards.front: What does fovea centralis have in the eye? What are the other two receptor fields
cards.back:
Maximal visual acuity and highest receptor density 

the next is the macular zone; and the outermost is the temporal crescent.

cards.front: What is anisocoria?
cards.back: It's a difference in pupil diameter, up to 1mm is normal

cards.front: When do you get a fixed and dilated pupil?
cards.back:
uncal hernation 
Dilation occurs first then the position change because parasympathetic fibers mediating pupillary constriction are at the periphery of CN III and are affected first by compressive lesions

cards.front: What should you consider in the ddx for ptosis?
cards.back:
If with ipsilateral pupillary dilation - CN III palsy 
If with ipsilateral pupillary constriction - Horner's syndrome

cards.front: What does pilocarpine do?
cards.back: It's an Ach agonist that should constrict any pupil unless dilated by atropine

cards.front: What is near light dissocation? What conditions can it be seen in?
cards.back:
failure of pupillary constriction to light, but preserved to accommodation 

1) Parinaud's syndrome (with pineal region lesions) 
2) MS 
3) alcoholism 
4) diabetes 
5) Argyll Robertson pupils 
6) Adie's syndrome

cards.front: What is retinitis pigmentosa?
cards.back:
affects children and adolescents, with male predominance and autosomal

recessive or dominant inheritance (chromosome 3). 

It causes bilateral degeneration of all layers of the retina with foveal sparing.

Symptoms include decreased night vision progressing to blindness.

Associated with multiple diseases (Refsum’s disease, Leber’s disease, etc.).

cards.front: Review your visual field deficits
Describe parinaud's syndrome
cards.back:
impaired upgaze
impaired convergence
mydriasis
convergence nystagmus,

lid retraction (Collier’s sign)
dissociated near light response can also be present 

Results from damage to dorsal midbrain and tectum from tumor, MS, or stroke

cards.front: What are causes of upgaze palsy
cards.back:
Parinaud's 
Niemann Pick's 
aging 
increased ICP

cards.front: Describe an INO lesion
cards.back:
caused by ipsilateral MLF lesion rostral to the abducens nucleus:

a. The ipsilateral eye does not adduct completely when looking to the contralateral side.
b. The contralateral eye exhibits nystagmus as it abducts.
c. MS is the most common cause in the young.
d. Bilateral INO causes bilateral adduction weakness.

e. Anterior INO caused by a lesion in the high midbrain. There is decreased convergence with bilateral adduction weakness.

f. Posterior INO caused by a pontine lesion. There is normal convergence with bilateral adduction weakness. Convergence occurs by means of paths to the CN III nuclei that are not in the MLF.

cards.front: Describe one and a half syndrome.
cards.back:
INO on one side and horizontal gaze palsy on the other secondary to bilat-

eral MLF and unilateral CN VI nucleus or nerve palsy:

a. One eye is fixed at midline for horizontal movements and one eye can only abduct.
b. Caused by vascular or demyelinating disease.

cards.front: What is Gradenigo's Syndrome?
cards.back: Associated with bacterial otitis media . Triad of periorbital/facial pain (CNV), diplopia (CNVI), and persistent otorrhea.

cards.front: What is the Gradenigo syndrome triad?
cards.back:
1) 6th CN palsy
2) retro orbital pain
3) draining ear (caused by otitis media) with mastoiditis and petrous apex involvement

cards.front: What is Tolosa-Hunt syndrome?
cards.back: inflammation of superior orbital fissure or cavernous sinus, painful ophthalmoplegia with multiple CN palsies, but pupil is spared; dramatic improvement with steroids

cards.front: Adie's syndrome
cards.back: degeneration of the ciliary ganglia and postganglionic parasympathetics (responsible for pupillary constriction). **more common in females, responds better to accommodation than light. Responds to 0.1% pilocarpine.

cards.front: What is Foster-kennedy syndrome?
cards.back: A large olfactory groove meningioma with decreased olfaction, ipsilateral optic nerve atrophy, and contralateral papilledema. Contralateral papilledema is from increased ICP, while ipsilateral atrophy is from direct mass effect.

cards.front: What is another name for a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD)? How is it detected?
cards.back: A Marcus gunn pupil - caused by optic neuritis or compression of a single optic nerve. Detected by the swinging flashlight test

cards.front: Describe the classic findings in Foster-Kennedy syndrome
cards.back: olfactory groove meningioma, ipsilateral anosmia, ipsilateral optic atrophy, contralateral papilledema

Lisch nodules
NF1
This is a retinal hamartoma

cards.front: What is this? What is a sign of?
cards.back:
Choroidal hemangioma 
Sign of sturge weber

cards.front: What is this and what is a sign of?
cards.back:
Ocular telangectasia 
Sign of ataxia telangectasia

cards.front: What is this and what does it mean?
cards.back:
Retinal hemorrhages
non accidental trauma in peds

cards.front: What is this?
cards.back:
Terson syndrome = intraocular / vitreous
hemorrhage a/w SAH

cards.front: What is this and what disease is it associated with?
cards.back:
Retinitis pigmentosa: a/w Refsum
disease (leukodystrophy)

cards.front: What is this?
cards.back: papilledema

cards.front: What is this sign and what is it associated with?
cards.back:
Argyll Robertson pupils = Near-light
dissociation, big b/l pupils w/o
light response, but appropriately constrict to accommodation

Occurs with pineal/tectal
tumors, teritary syphilis, Wernicke encephalopathy
Kayser Fleischer rings - Wilson disease (copper deposition)
fibrous dysplasia

Orbital cavernous hemangioma (most common
adult orbital lesion, melanoma is most common tumor)

cards.front: The presence of a Marcus Gunn pupil in association with multiple sclerosis indicates that the lesion is in which of the following locations?
cards.back:
Optic nerve
Relative afferent pupillary defect 
Illicited by a swinging flashlight test - aberrant pupillary dilatation upon light stimulation of the ipsilateral eye
Impaired action of the pupillary light reflex - stimulation of the retina triggers the optic nerve, ipsilateral optic tract, and bilateral pretectal nuclei, which then synapse upon the bilateral III and ciliary ganglion, resulting in iris contraction

cards.front: A 30 year old woman struck her forehead in a motor vehicle accident and she did not lose consciousness. Examination in the dark revealed a right pupil of 6 mm that was sluggishly reactive to light and a left pupil of 4 mm that was briskly reactive to light. There was no ptosis or ocular motility disturbance. Thirty minutes after instillation into the eyes of dilute pilocarpine, her right pupil was 2 mm and the left was 4 mm. What pupil abnormality is most likely?
cards.back:
Adie's pupil 

This patient has sensitivity to dilute pilocarpine, which is a hallmark feature of the benign Adie's pupil that occurs more commonly in women in their early fourth decade of life.

When mydriatics are instilled into the eye, as rarely happens in a case of medical malingering or Munchausen's, the pupil becomes sluggish or tonic and does not respond to even concentrated pilocarpine. A traumatic mydriasis would not show supersensitivity to dilute pilocarpine and would also likely show a rupture of the iris.

cards.front: What are ocular manifestations of CCF?
cards.back: Ocular manifestations of a carotid cavernous fistula (CCF) include eye redness (conjunctival hyperemia), bulging eye (proptosis), eyelid swelling (ptosis), double vision (diplopia), reduced vision, ophthalmoplegia, and an elevated eye pressure (glaucoma).
